verses.commentary: Jesus calls the Twelve, gives them power (dynamis, δύναμις) and authority (exousia, ἐξουσία) over all demons and to cure diseases. Both terms — capability and right. Delegated ministry begins.
Sends them out to proclaim the kingdom of God and to heal the sick. The two activities together — word and deed inseparable.
'Take nothing for the journey — no staff, no bag, no bread, no money, no extra tunic.' Total dependence on hospitality. The mission flies light.
Stay in whatever house receives you — until you leave that town. Avoid hospitality-hunting. Stable base.
If they do not receive you — shake the dust off your feet as a testimony against them. The judgment-marker.
They went through villages preaching the gospel and healing everywhere. Mission executed.
Herod the tetrarch hears all that was happening. Perplexed (dieporei, διηπόρει) — because some said John was raised, some Elijah, some another prophet of old.
Various theories circulate — all of them inadequate. Popular Christology fails to identify Jesus.
Herod: 'John I beheaded; but who is this about whom I hear such things?' He sought to see him. Curiosity laced with guilt.
Apostles return, tell Jesus what they have done. He takes them, withdraws privately to Bethsaida. Decompression and debriefing.
Crowds learn and follow. He welcomes them, speaks about the kingdom, heals those needing healing. Privacy yields to compassion.
Late afternoon. Twelve: 'Send the crowd away to find food and lodging in surrounding villages.' Pragmatic dismissal proposed.
'You give them something to eat.' Same as Matthew 14:16. 'We have no more than five loaves and two fish — unless we go and buy food.' Inventory and shortfall named.
About five thousand men. 'Have them sit down in groups of fifty.' Organized seating — pastoral and practical.
They do so. All sit down. Order precedes provision.
Took the five loaves and two fish, looked up to heaven, blessed and broke them, gave to disciples. The fourfold action recurs — eucharistic foreshadowing.
All ate and were satisfied. Twelve baskets of broken pieces left over. Surplus from scarcity.
Once while praying alone (Lukan distinctive — Jesus at prayer), disciples were with him. 'Who do the crowds say I am?' Pivotal question framed in prayer.
Same answers as 9:7-8 — John, Elijah, one of the old prophets risen.
'But you — who do you say I am?' Peter: 'The Christ of God.' Confession given.
Strictly charged them to tell no one. Messianic secret preserved before the cross. Premature acclamation distorts.

'The Son of Man must (dei, δεῖ) suffer many things, be rejected by the elders, chief priests, scribes, and be killed, and on the third day be raised.' First passion prediction. Necessity, not accident.
'If anyone would come after me, let him deny himself and take up his cross daily (kath' hemeran, καθ' ἡμέραν — Lukan addition) and follow me.' Daily cross-bearing. Discipleship as repeated dying.
Save life — lose it. Lose life for my sake — save it. Paradox stated.
Gain the whole world and lose or forfeit himself — what good? Cost-benefit eternally favoring the soul.
Whoever is ashamed of me and my words — the Son of Man will be ashamed of him when he comes in his glory and the Father's and the holy angels'. Eschatological reciprocity.
'Some standing here will not taste death before they see the kingdom of God.' Likely points forward to the transfiguration.
About eight days later — took Peter, John, James — went up the mountain to pray. The pattern again: prayer and revelation paired.
As he was praying, the appearance of his face was altered, his clothing dazzling white. Glory broke through during prayer.
Two men, Moses and Elijah, appeared in glory. Law and Prophets confer with Jesus.
They spoke of his departure (exodos, ἔξοδον — exodus) which he was about to accomplish at Jerusalem. Lukan unique detail. Jesus' death is a new exodus — liberation accomplished in Jerusalem.
Peter and his companions were heavy with sleep but, becoming fully awake, saw his glory and the two men with him. Drowsiness yields to vision.

As they were parting from Jesus, Peter says, 'Master, it is good for us to be here; let us make three tents — one for you, one for Moses, one for Elijah.' Not knowing what he said. The misstep is honest.
While he was saying this, a cloud came and overshadowed them. They were afraid as they entered the cloud. Sinai-shekinah imagery.

Voice from the cloud: 'This is my Son, my Chosen One; listen to him.' Lukan 'Chosen One' (eklelegmenos, ἐκλελεγμένος) instead of 'beloved.' Election-language. 'Listen to him' — Deuteronomy 18:15 echo.
After the voice — only Jesus. Disciples kept silent, told no one in those days what they had seen. Internal restraint.
Next day — coming down from the mountain — a great crowd met him. Glory above, need below.
Man cries: 'Teacher, I beg you to look upon my son, my only child.' Third Lukan 'only child' (compare 7:12, 8:42).
Spirit seizes him; suddenly screams, convulses, foaming at the mouth; will scarcely leave him, mauls him cruelly.
'I begged your disciples to cast it out, but they could not.' The disciples' inability exposed publicly.
'O faithless and twisted generation, how long am I to be with you and bear with you? Bring your son here.' Frustration with persistent unbelief.
As he was coming, the demon threw him down and convulsed him. Jesus rebuked the unclean spirit, healed the boy, and gave him back to his father. Family restoration imagery.
All astonished at the majesty (megaleioteti, μεγαλειότητι) of God. Marveling at all that Jesus was doing — he said to disciples:

'Let these words sink into your ears — the Son of Man is about to be delivered (paradidosthai, παραδίδοσθαι) into the hands of men.' Second passion prediction — counterpoint to the glory just witnessed.
They did not understand this saying — it was concealed (parakekalymmenon, παρακεκαλυμμένον) from them so they would not perceive it; they were afraid to ask. Comprehension blocked, fear of asking.
An argument arose among them as to who was the greatest. Status competition in the face of cross-talk.
Jesus, knowing their hearts' reasoning, took a child and put him by his side. Visual rebuke.

'Whoever receives this child in my name receives me; whoever receives me receives him who sent me. For he who is least among you all is the great one.' Reception of small = reception of Christ. Greatness inverted.
John: 'Master, we saw someone casting out demons in your name — we tried to stop him, because he does not follow with us.' Sectarianism on display.
'Do not stop him; whoever is not against you is for you.' Open posture toward those operating in Jesus' name. Sect-boundary lower than they assumed.

When the days drew near for him to be taken up (analempseos, ἀναλήμψεως — taking-up, ascension language), 'he set his face (esterisen to prosopon, ἐστήρισεν τὸ πρόσωπον — fixed his face firmly) to go to Jerusalem.' Lukan pivot. The journey-to-Jerusalem narrative begins here (extending to 19:27). Theological compass set.
Sends messengers ahead — they enter a Samaritan village to prepare for him.
Samaritans did not receive him because his face was set toward Jerusalem. Jewish-Samaritan tension over worship-site (Mount Gerizim vs. Jerusalem) explodes here.
James and John see this: 'Lord, do you want us to tell fire to come down from heaven and consume them?' Elijah-style judgment requested (2 Kings 1:10). Sons of Thunder living up to their nickname.
Jesus turns and rebukes them. The 'new Elijah' refuses Elijah-style fire. Different mission.
They went on to another village. Rejection met with re-direction, not retaliation.
Along the road, a man: 'I will follow you wherever you go.' Enthusiastic volunteer.
'Foxes have holes, birds of the air have nests; the Son of Man has nowhere to lay his head.' Discipleship as homelessness.
To another: 'Follow me.' Reply: 'Lord, let me first go bury my father.' Standard filial duty.
'Leave the dead to bury their own dead; you go and proclaim the kingdom of God.' Kingdom priority overrides even foundational obligation. The dead can handle the dead.
Yet another: 'I will follow, but let me first say farewell at home.' Reasonable request.
'No one who puts his hand to the plow and looks back is fit for the kingdom of God.' Plowing requires forward focus; looking back ruins the furrow. Discipleship is a forward-only orientation.
